1.synthesis: The fifth angel sounds; a star fallen from heaven opens the shaft of the bottomless pit, releasing smoke 'like the smoke of a great furnace, and the sun and the air were darkened with the smoke from the shaft' (v.2). From the smoke come locusts with power like scorpions, commanded not to harm green things but only 'those people who do not have the seal of God on their foreheads' (v.4). They torture for five months but cannot kill; 'people will seek death and will not find it. They will long to die, but death will flee from them' (v.6). Their king is the angel of the bottomless pit, 'whose name in Hebrew is Abaddon, and in Greek he is called Apollyon' (v.11) — both names meaning Destroyer.
1.historical_context: The fifth trumpet is the first 'woe' (v.12). The 'star fallen from heaven' (v.1) echoes Isaiah 14:12 (the fall of the king of Babylon, 'Lucifer, son of the morning') and anticipates 12:9 (Satan cast down). The bottomless pit (abyssos) is the realm of demonic captivity — the same place where the Gerasene demons begged not to be sent (Luke 8:31). The locust imagery draws from Joel 1:6 and Joel 2:4–9, where locusts are described as a mighty army with horses' faces and flowing hair — exactly the description of the apocalyptic locusts in vv.7–9. The name 'Apollyon' (Destroyer) in Greek is suggestive: the emperor Domitian identified himself with Apollo, the sun god; the angel of the pit named 'Apollo's destroying counterpart' may carry implicit anti-imperial irony. The five months of torment (v.5) matches the lifespan of ordinary locusts — bounded, temporary, not ultimate.
1.literary_structure: The fifth trumpet is the most elaborately described of the trumpets: the first four receive brief notices, while the fifth occupies twelve verses of detailed imagery. The demonic locust-army is described with precise detail that defies literal interpretation and demands symbolic reading — human faces, women's hair, lions' teeth, iron breastplates, wings roaring like chariots and horses prepared for battle (vv.7–9). The composite description makes the point that these forces defy natural categorization: they are demonic, not merely natural. The crucial distinction between the sealed and the unsealed (v.4: cannot touch those with God's seal) recapitulates the interlude of ch.7 and reinforces it: the protective seal of God is real protection against the demonic assault of the fifth trumpet. The first woe is 'past' at v.12 before the next is announced — the narrative carefully marks the sequence.
1.theological_application: Gill on the bottomless pit: 'This is a place of the greatest darkness and misery, called the deep, or abyss, the pit that has no bottom, than which nothing can be more dreadful and terrible; and fitly represents the state of those who are under the power and influence of the devil, in the darkness of Popery and superstition, in the depth of wickedness and misery, in all the horrors of a legal and a guilty conscience.' Barnes on the seal of protection (v.4): 'The idea here is, that those who are the true friends of God would be safe amid these calamities. This was the case in the plagues of Egypt, where the Israelites were protected while the Egyptians were afflicted.' The torment but not death (v.5) is one of the judgment's most chilling features: those who seek death cannot find it. This is the exact inverse of the martyrs' experience (6:11), who die but whose deaths are preserved and avenged — the earth-dwellers who die for the beast's sake lose even the option of escaping suffering through death.
1.christology: The key of the bottomless pit is given to the fallen star (v.1) — but Christ holds 'the keys of Death and Hades' (1:18). The demonic locusts operate under the authority of Abaddon/Apollyon (v.11), yet Abaddon is a prisoner in the pit rather than its sovereign: the key that opened it came from outside. The five-month limitation (v.5) on the locusts' torment is the christological constraint: demonic forces cannot exceed the boundaries set by the one who holds the keys. The protection of the sealed (v.4) confirms that the Lamb's seal is effective against the pit's forces — those who bear God's mark cannot be touched by those who bear the destroyer's authority. The christological claim is implicit but structural: the pit is opened and bounded by the sovereign purposes of the Lamb who opened the seal that disclosed this trumpet.

13.synthesis: The sixth angel sounds; a voice from the altar commands the release of 'the four angels who are bound at the great river Euphrates' (v.14), released for 'the hour, the day, the month, and the year, to kill a third of mankind' (v.15). John hears the cavalry: 200 million. The horses have 'heads like lions, and fire and smoke and sulfur came out of their mouths. By these three plagues a third of mankind was killed, by the fire and smoke and sulfur coming out of their mouths' (vv.17–18). The rest of mankind, not killed by these plagues, 'did not repent of the works of their hands nor give up worshiping demons and idols of gold and silver and bronze and stone and wood … nor did they repent of their murders or their sorceries or their sexual immorality or their thefts' (vv.20–21).
13.historical_context: The four angels bound at the Euphrates (v.14) evoke the eastern border of the Roman Empire, beyond which lay Parthia — Rome's most feared adversary and the source of its greatest military anxiety. The Euphrates was the psychological boundary of the civilized world for Romans; the Parthian cavalry were among the most feared in the ancient world. The 200 million horsemen (literally 'two myriads of myriads') represent a force so vast as to be cosmic in scale — no ancient army of this size existed or could exist. The fire, smoke, and sulfur from the horses' mouths (v.17) are the three elements of Sodom's destruction (Gen 19:24–28) and the covenant curses of Deuteronomy 29:23. The chilling conclusion (vv.20–21) is that a third of humanity dying in the sixth-trumpet judgment produces no repentance — the earth-dwellers continue in idol-worship, murder, sorcery, immorality, and theft. The catalog of sins matches precisely the seven deadly sins associated with idolatry in the prophets (Deut 18:10–11).
13.literary_structure: The sixth trumpet is the second woe and the most violent of the trumpet series — a third of humanity is killed, compared to the fifth trumpet's torment without death. The escalation from the first four trumpets (a third of creation damaged) to the fifth (torment of the unsealed) to the sixth (death of a third of humanity) marks the intensification of judgment as the scroll's purposes unfold. The narrative's most devastating moment is vv.20–21: after all this, no repentance. The purpose of the judgments — to lead to repentance and recognition of God — has failed in its persuasive function for the earth-dwellers. This prepares the theological ground for the bowls (chs.15–16) which will no longer be partial warnings but full expressions of divine wrath. The sixth trumpet, like the sixth seal, creates a penultimate crisis that is resolved not immediately but through an interlude (chs.10–11) before the seventh trumpet sounds.
13.theological_application: Barnes on the no-repentance conclusion (v.20): 'Perhaps nothing could more strikingly show the power of idolatry over the mind than this. Men had seen one plague after another visited on the guilty, and yet they clung to their idols. The whole history of idolatry shows how strong a hold it has on the human heart, and how difficult it is to break its chains.' Gill on 'did not repent of their murders' (v.21): 'Though such instances of divine wrath had been upon the world, they did not reform from their evil ways; particularly from their murders, in shedding the blood of the saints.' The sixth trumpet's no-repentance conclusion is one of Revelation's most theologically sobering passages: it testifies that divine judgment, however severe, does not automatically produce repentance. The hardening that occurred in Pharaoh (despite the Exodus plagues) is not unique to Pharaoh — it is the pattern of the human heart that has chosen the beast's mark over God's seal. Repentance is not a response that judgment compels; it requires the gift of the Spirit.
13.christology: The precise timing of the four angels' release — 'the hour, the day, the month, and the year' (v.15) — establishes that the judgment of the sixth trumpet is not random but occurs at the exact moment determined by divine decree. This precision echoes the 'hour' language that Christ uses in the Gospels for the cross ('my hour has not yet come,' John 2:4) and that Revelation reserves for the moments of divine appointment. The sixth trumpet's judgment, like the cross, happens at the time appointed by the Father. The idols catalogued in v.20 — 'gold and silver and bronze and stone and wood' — parallel Daniel 5:23 (Belshazzar's gods) and represent the entire spectrum of material religion opposed to the worship of the one on the throne and the Lamb. The earth-dwellers' refusal to repent is their final choice of the idols over the Christ who was slain for them — a choice whose consequences the bowl judgments will consummate.
